title: Handle system sleep and wake

contextToReadFirst.path: Apple docs on NSWorkspace.willSleepNotification and didWakeNotification
contextToReadFirst.reason: FSEvents may or may not deliver events that occurred during sleep — behavior varies by macOS version

changes: On wake, perform a directory scan: list all .m4a files in the watched directory, compare against `baselineFiles` plus all files already emitted (track emitted files in a `private var emittedFiles: Set<URL>`), emit any files that are new and not already processed
After the wake scan, the FSEvents stream should continue monitoring normally for subsequent files
Subscribe to `willSleepNotification` — log a debug message but no special action needed. The FSEvents stream survives sleep.

tests.name: test_wakeScanDetectsNewFiles
tests.description: Simulate a wake scan by calling the internal scan method directly. Add a .m4a file to the temp directory without going through FSEvents (simulating a file that synced during sleep). Call the wake scan. Assert onFileDetected fires for that file.

tests.name: test_wakeScanDoesNotReemitPreviousFiles
tests.description: Emit a file normally through FSEvents, then trigger a wake scan. Assert the already-emitted file is not emitted again.

manualTestProcedure: Start Utterd. Put Mac to sleep. Record a voice memo on iPhone. Wait 2 minutes for iCloud sync. Wake the Mac. Observe logs to confirm the new memo is detected after wake.
doneWhen: After system wake, any .m4a files that synced during sleep are detected and emitted. Previously emitted files are not re-emitted. Manual test procedure documented.
doNot: Stop and restart the FSEvents stream on wake — it should survive sleep. Only supplement it with a directory scan.
Rely solely on FSEvents to catch files that synced during sleep — the scan is the safety net
